title: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis | ALS | Lou Gehrig's disease | MedlinePlus
content: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis Also called: ALS, Lou Gehrig's disease On this page Basics Summary Start Here Symptoms Diagnosis and Tests Prevention and Risk Factors Learn More Living With Related Issues Genetics See, Play and Learn No links available Research Statistics and Research Clinical Trials Journal Articles Resources Find an Expert For You Children Patient Handouts Summary Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) is a nervous system disease that attacks nerve cells called neurons in your brain and spinal cord. These neurons transmit messages from your brain and spinal cord to your voluntary muscles - the ones you can control, like in your arms and legs. At first, this causes mild muscle problems. Some people notice: Trouble walking or running Trouble writing Speech problems Eventually, you lose your strength and cannot move. When muscles in your chest fail, you cannot breathe. A breathing machine can help, but most people with ALS die from respiratory failure . The disease usually strikes between age 40 and 60. More men than women get it. No one knows what causes ALS. It can run in families, but usually it strikes at random. There is no cure. Medicines can relieve symptoms and, sometimes, prolong survival.
